original_hypothesis.title: WAVAX Withdrawals DoS for Smart Contracts via .transfer
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The use of the legacy `.transfer` method prevents smart contracts with complex receive logic from withdrawing their WAVAX.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: `WAVAX.withdraw` uses `payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad)`, which forwards a strict 2300 gas stipend. Any smart contract wallet or multisig that consumes more than 2300 gas in its fallback/receive function will be permanently unable to unwrap its WAVAX.
preconditions: A smart contract with a complex receive function holds WAVAX

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Use of deprecated .transfer method for ETH/AVAX transfers

operational_questions: Are protocol multisigs or operational contracts holding WAVAX?
validation_requirements: Test WAVAX withdrawal to a contract that consumes > 2300 gas

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Smart contract wallet deposits AVAX to get WAVAX
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts due to 2300 gas limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds are locked in WAVAX contract

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires transferring WAVAX to an EOA first

validation_result.impact_summary: Smart contracts with receive/fallback functions consuming >2300 gas cannot withdraw WAVAX, forcing a workaround via ERC-20 transfer to an EOA.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The WAVAX.sol `withdraw` function uses `payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad)` on its last line. The Solidity `.transfer` method forwards a fixed 2300 gas stipend to the recipient. Any smart contract calling `withdraw` whose `receive()` or `fallback()` function requires more than 2300 gas (e.g., multisig wallets, proxy contracts, contracts that emit events or write storage in their receive function) will cause the transfer to revert. Since `.transfer` reverts on failure (does not return false), the entire `withdraw` transaction reverts, meaning the caller's WAVAX balance is restored but they are unable to unwrap their WAVAX through the standard `withdraw` mechanism. This is a well-documented anti-pattern (SWC-134 / Consensys best practices). The recommended approach is to use `(bool success, ) = payable(msg.sender).call{value: wad}(""); require(success, ...)` which forwards all available gas. While funds are not permanently lost (the smart contract can transfer WAVAX as an ERC-20 token to an EOA which can then withdraw), the `withdraw` function does not work correctly for a class of valid callers, constituting a code defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal points to WAVAX.sol line 34 with pattern `payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad);`. Examining the source code confirms this exact line exists in the `withdraw` function. The `.transfer` call is the sole mechanism for returning native AVAX to the caller, and it imposes a strict 2300 gas limit on the recipient's execution context.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The use of `payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad)` forwards only 2300 gas. Smart contracts with receive/fallback functions that consume more than 2300 gas (proxy contracts, multisigs, contracts with event emissions or storage writes in receive) will cause this call to revert, preventing them from unwrapping their WAVAX.

calling_sequence: 6. Smart contract's receive() function runs with only 2300 gas
7. If receive() needs >2300 gas, transfer reverts
8. Entire withdraw transaction reverts, balance restored but AVAX not returned
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The complete path is: smart contract → WAVAX.withdraw(wad) → balance check → balance decrement → events → payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad) → recipient receive() with 2300 gas → revert if gas insufficient. The `.transfer` on the last line of withdraw (WAVAX.sol approximately line 34) is the defect point.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts specifically for WAVAX were provided. The contract is a standalone deployment with no configuration dependencies.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is straightforward and realistic: (1) A multisig wallet, proxy contract, or any smart contract with a non-trivial receive function deposits AVAX into WAVAX. (2) When the contract tries to call withdraw(), the payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad) fails because the recipient's receive() function exceeds 2300 gas. (3) The withdraw transaction reverts. (4) The smart contract's WAVAX is effectively locked in wrapped form - it can only be moved via ERC-20 transfer to an EOA which can then unwrap. This is not a theoretical concern; many production smart contract wallets (Gnosis Safe, etc.) have receive functions that exceed 2300 gas.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. Smart contracts holding WAVAX cannot directly unwrap it via the withdraw function. Funds are not permanently lost since WAVAX can be transferred as an ERC-20 token to an EOA that can then unwrap. However, this breaks the expected functionality for an important class of users (smart contract wallets, multisigs, DeFi protocols). The workaround adds friction and may cause issues for automated systems that expect withdraw to succeed.
recommended_mitigations: Replace `payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad)` with a low-level call: `(bool success, ) = payable(msg.sender).call{value: wad}(""); require(success, "WAVAX: AVAX transfer failed");`
This forwards all available gas to the recipient, allowing smart contracts with complex receive functions to successfully receive AVAX
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy WAVAX contract. 2. Deploy a test contract with a receive() function that writes to storage (costs >2300 gas). 3. From the test contract, call WAVAX.deposit{value: 1 ether}(). 4. From the test contract, call WAVAX.withdraw(1 ether). 5. Observe that the withdraw transaction reverts with an out-of-gas error in the transfer call.
uncertainties: The exact gas cost of receive() functions in specific production wallets varies, but the 2300 gas limit is well-established as insufficient for most non-trivial operations
Whether the protocol team considers this contract immutable or if it can be redeployed
additional_testing_needed: Test with Gnosis Safe as the caller to confirm DoS
Test with common proxy patterns (EIP-1967, UUPS) as callers
Verify that RouterV2's usage of WAVAX (via wETH.withdraw) is unaffected since RouterV2 has a simple receive() that only asserts msg.sender == address(wETH)
